title: AI Won't Kill Jobs — It Will Explode the Problem Space
body_html:
The dominant narrative around AI and employment treats the economy as a fixed pie of tasks being transferred from humans to machines. This model is not just wrong — it is backwards. The actual experience of working intensively with AI tools tells a completely different story: every problem solved reveals multiple new problems that were previously invisible or impractical to tackle. The backlog doesn't shrink. It detonates.

This pattern is not new. Antibiotics solved bacterial infection and created antibiotic resistance as an entire field of research. The internet solved information access and spawned misinformation, attention economics, and cybersecurity. The automobile solved personal transportation and generated traffic engineering, urban planning, insurance law, and emissions regulation. The expansion is combinatorial — each solution branches into multiple new challenges. AI, as the most powerful problem-solving accelerator ever built, will make this branching happen faster than it ever has before.

The people closest to the technology confirm this daily. Practitioners working with AI report not that they are running out of things to do, but that they are overwhelmed by how much suddenly can be done. Tuesday's solution creates Wednesday's three new projects. A million problems that used to be impractical to solve are now solvable, and once you see that, you cannot stop. Productivity goes through the roof, and so does the quantity of meaningful work available.

The material improvements will be real and significant. The price of goods and services will drop. Things that are expensive now will become cheap. Things that are impossible now will become routine. But history shows what happens next: humans recalibrate to the new baseline and find new things to work on and worry about. A person from 1300 transported to today would think they had arrived in paradise — abundant food, clean water, medicine, flight, a pocket device containing most of human knowledge. It doesn't feel like paradise. It feels like Tuesday. The same recalibration will happen with AI's gains.

What endures through every technological revolution is the need for human judgment about which newly accessible problems actually matter. Moravec's paradox — that evolution's oldest capacities are the hardest for machines to replicate — suggests the most durable human skills are not the cognitive feats AI handles easily, like arithmetic and search, but the ancient ones: contextual interpretation, gut feeling, embodied sensory judgment, and the ability to care about outcomes. The future of work is not less work. It is different work, more work, and work that leans harder than ever on what makes us human.
